sentences.base_language: I need to go to the hospital.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜야 하다 / 〜아야 하다 (ya hada) = 'must / have to': 가야 해요 (must go), 먹어야 해요 (must eat). It's the standard way to express obligation in Korean. The negative 〜지 않아도 되다 means 'don't have to'.

sentences.base_language: My throat hurts and I have a fever.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜고 (go) connects two clauses as a list or sequence: 아프고 (hurts and...), 피곤하고 (tired and...). It's neutral — it doesn't imply cause or contrast, just addition. Body part + 이/가 + 아프다 is the core pain expression pattern.

sentences.base_language: Since when have you been sick?

sentences.grammarNote: 부터 (buteo) = 'from / since' (a starting point in time): 어제부터 (since yesterday), 아침부터 (since morning). Its counterpart 까지 (kkaji) = 'until / up to': 내일까지 (until tomorrow). Together: 월요일부터 금요일까지 (Monday through Friday).

〜아/어 주세요 (a/eo juseyo) = 'please do [something] for me'. It softens a request by framing the action as a favor: 벌려 주세요 (please open [it]), 가르쳐 주세요 (please teach me). This is the standard polite request form in Korean.

sentences.base_language: It's a cold. I'll give you medicine.
sentences.grammarNote: 드리다 (deurida) is the humble form of 주다 (to give) — used when the speaker gives something to someone of higher status: 드릴게요 (I will give [to you/them]). This shows respect to the patient as the recipient.

sentences.base_language: Pick up the medicine at the pharmacy.
